title: Reading and Use of English – Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about the history of the camera. Choose from paragraphs A–G which fits each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.articleTitle: Capturing the Moment: The Evolution of Photography
content.text: The desire to freeze a moment in time is a fundamental human impulse, but for centuries, it was limited to the skill of a painter's brush. The journey toward the modern camera began not with a lens, but with a simple hole in a darkened room. Known as the *camera obscura*, this device projected an inverted image of the outside world onto a wall. ||(41)|| [41]This transition from a fleeting projection to a permanent record was achieved in 1826 by Joseph Nicéphore Niépce[/41]. He used a polished pewter plate coated with a light-sensitive chemical called bitumen. By exposing the plate in a camera obscura for eight hours, he produced the world's first surviving photograph: a grainy view of his courtyard. ||(42)|| [42]This 'heliography' was revolutionary, but impractical due to the incredibly long exposure times[/42]. The breakthrough that made photography a commercial reality came from Niépce’s partner, Louis Daguerre. In 1839, he introduced the 'daguerreotype,' which reduced exposure time to under thirty minutes and produced a sharp, mirrored image on a silvered copper plate. ||(43)|| [43]However, the daguerreotype was a 'dead end' in terms of reproduction; each plate was a unique object that could not be copied[/43]. The solution came from William Henry Fox Talbot, who developed the 'calotype' process. By using a paper negative, Talbot made it possible to print an unlimited number of positive copies, laying the foundation for the modern photographic industry. ||(44)|| [44]As the 19th century progressed, the 'wet plate' process made photography faster but also more cumbersome[/44]. Photographers had to carry a portable darkroom to coat and develop their glass plates on the spot before the chemicals dried. This changed in 1888 when George Eastman introduced the Kodak camera. ||(45)|| [45]This democratization of the image transformed society[/45]. For the first time, ordinary families could document their own lives without the need for a professional studio. Photography moved from the laboratory to the street, capturing the raw, unposed reality of human existence. ||(46)|| [46]Today, we carry powerful digital cameras in our pockets, capable of taking thousands of high-resolution images in a single day[/46]. While the medium has shifted from chemicals to sensors, the underlying goal remains the same: to stop the clock and hold onto a sliver of time that would otherwise be lost forever.

paragraphs.C: Indeed, [41] this optical phenomenon [/41] had been known since antiquity, used by artists like Vermeer to achieve perfect perspective. However, the image disappeared as soon as the light changed, leaving scientists to wonder if there was a way to 'fix' the picture onto a physical surface.
paragraphs.A: This [42] primitive image [/42] was barely visible, but it proved that light could be used to create a permanent physical change in a material. It sparked a race among inventors across Europe to find more sensitive chemicals that could capture an image in minutes rather than hours.
paragraphs.G: The [43] popularity of the daguerreotype [/43] was immediate. People flocked to have their portraits taken, though they had to sit perfectly still in bright sunlight for several minutes, often using metal head-rests to prevent any blurring of the final image.
paragraphs.B: This [44] shift to the negative-positive system [/44] was the most important technical advancement in the history of the medium. It turned photography from a scientific curiosity into a mass-communication tool, allowing for the birth of photojournalism and the illustrated press.
paragraphs.D: The [45] genius of the Kodak [/45] was its simplicity. With the slogan 'You press the button, we do the rest,' Eastman provided a camera pre-loaded with a roll of flexible film. Once the 100 pictures were taken, the user sent the entire camera back to the factory for developing.
paragraphs.F: This [46] transition to digital technology [/46] began in the 1970s with the invention of the CCD sensor. By converting light into electrical signals, it eliminated the need for film and darkrooms, leading to the instant gratification of the smartphone era.
paragraphs.E: Many [47] traditional darkroom enthusiasts [/47] still argue that digital sensors lack the 'organic' feel of film. They claim that the chemical process adds a layer of depth and texture to a photograph that a collection of pixels can never truly replicate.

questions.explanation: C identifies the 'optical phenomenon' of the camera obscura and explains the desire to 'fix' the disappearing image.
A refers back to Niépce’s 'primitive image' (the view of the courtyard) and the race to find better chemicals.
G describes the 'popularity of the daguerreotype' and the difficulty of sitting still for the exposure, following Daguerre's introduction.
B refers to Talbot's 'negative-positive system' and its impact on the mass-communication of images.
D explains the simplicity of the 'Kodak camera' and Eastman's revolutionary 'you press the button' approach.
F bridges the gap between the film era and the 'digital technology' mentioned in the final paragraph.
